item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a fan of this. Minor pet peeve stuff (UIU getting a Junior Deputy SCP Super Secret Agent badge, letter at the end, even though it did have some narrative functionality), combined with an interview log that, as far as I can tell, does nothing to advance the plot and essentially reiterates the description, makes for a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just can't in good conscience upvote a skip that relies in its comments to "explain it". I didn't quite get what this thing did until I read a comment above my own. I know that overexplaining things takes away the magic and mystique, but writing confusing scips is also not a great merit. I can't see a way to fix this unless you are comfortable adding quite a lot of text, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off I didn't think the Foundation were in the business of creating scips.

Headcanon mileage may vary here, but given how the SCP Foundation in addition to containing anomalies constantly experiments on them, it's not so far out of line that they would create anomalies on occasion. In fact, we have an entire tag for such articles under foudnation-made. Articles such as SCP-2000, SCP-085, and no less than six SCP-001 proposals are included in this category.

While it is arguable that the SCP Foundation here was rather cavalier with their use of a congnitohazard of this caliber, it's also understandable why they would use it as such given it is particularly helpful in their mission of maintaining the veil of normalcy. Whether they should have anticipated someone like Vincent Anderson and Co. reverse engineering it to their own ends is likewise debatable given that such individuals are more few and far between than Joey Averageman whom the effect is intended.

That said, you're more than welcome to your own headcanon and to use it in measuring how you vote. You will find, however, that this SCP is more than in good company in its depiction of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scip was referred to me by ADream after I mentioned that with the frequency that the Foundation uses SCP as an acronym or abbreviation for it's front companies there must be conspiracy theories about it. Unfortunately much as I love the idea behind this it is sort of ruined by the fact that it originates with the Foundation. First off I didn't think the Foundation were in the business of creating scips. Next the concept that the Foundation would splash around an infohazard this potentially dangerous is a bit absurd. Finally the fact that someone on the O5 council hasn't come down on the genius that greenlit this project like a tonne of non-anomalous bricks greatly surprises me. Overall I like the idea as well as the logs but its origin ruins it for me. A reluctant-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing I liked here at all was the clever and bold move from Anderson of signing the letter that way. Yes, it makes sense to work at the same time that it is an extremely risky move.

But overall it failed for me. The Foundation implementing an infohazard as a common tool isn't something that sits well for me to begin with, and the fact that several tales have them weaponizing memetics doesn't solve it to me, I don't like this in those tales, either.

+ show block
– hide block

What really kills it in this regard, though, is the carelessness. The Foundation was able to detect a fair share of memes, infohazards and cognitohazards that were far subtler than this. Putting an infohazard above the door of every front company of them is the same as asking for all GOIs and POIs out there with any chance to learn it to, well, learn it.

You must remember that frequency is a thing. Even if the infohazard is subtle enough for the chance of it being found at a given time being very little, it will be there for enough time to people just figure it out eventually, it is a given. So it was a really dumb idea.

And the Foundation suddenly struggling to comprehend an infohazard of its own design is also dumb. A lot of space is dedicated to personnel discussing its effects to try and find what it does, but actually the Foundation already knows what it does, and their conversation doesn't really adds nothing to its understanding. And it shouldn't, again because it was a Foundation design. How it came to Anderson and how he's using it should be a more pressing matter.

Nothing here actually works for me. It is a pointless investigation on its effects, a pointless exchange between agents, and neither it origins in the Foundation, nor its use by Anderson, the actual good stories to be told, received proper attention. And the passing nature of their presence guarantees that they become also pointless, like as clever as it is, the letter in the end is ultimately pointless because the article doesn't give me reason to care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation makes a thing with the acronym SCP that turns affected material into an infohazard. It means that, if the plaque over the entrance of the building reads Swanson's Corporate Pub, they can convert it easily into an infohazard.

+ show block
– hide block

Once that is made, the effect of the infohazard is that anyone that knows the affected acronym becomes unable to figure out that the acronym is linked to the Foundation unless this is directly pointed ou to them. So anyone that sees the plaque will be unable to conclude by their own that this Pub is related to the Foundation, no matter how much evidence they see, until someone effectively tell them that it is related to the Foundation.

Since it is an infohazard and obscures relationship, it affects other things by proxy: anything you know to be related to the plaque will be affected, because if not you would be able to conclude that the plaque is related to the Foundation. So you won't conclude that the building is related, or that any of its regulars are related to the Foundation, because if they are, so there is a relationship between the plaque and the Foundation, and you can't reach such conclusion.

Finally it is worth to mention that this thing is an infohazard, so the "magic" isn't neither on the object affected, nor the visual of the symbol. It is in the very knowledge of the existence of the affected thing, so if I see the plaque, and then I describe the plaque to you, you become as unable to conclude the link as me, because you know the affected plaque, including the acronym. And then, if I describe the building and its regulars you'll also be affected, because they are still linked to the plaque in some way. But if I describe you the building and the regulars, but not the plaque, then you can conclude that they have some relation to the Foundation, normally, and you'll become able to tell me that. Likewise, if you learn of the plaque and the building, becoming affected, but meet the regulars outside this setup, you can conclude they are related to the Foundation.

It will bring the last weirdness: you'll know that people is linked to the Foundation, and after that you can learn that they are regulars in that Pub, but even then you'll be unable to conclude that the Pub itself is linked to the Foundation, unless someone actually tells you that.

Anderson changed the basic element from the SCP acronym to the symbol &, and the impossible link to himself. So if you see the affected &, you cannot conclude that that particular & is linked to Anderson in any way, and consequently you can't conclude that nothing linked to that & is linked to Anderson.

But the definitive proof of concept is the final letter. It uses the & to substitute for "And" in Anderson, so the letter itself becomes impossible to be linked to Anderson unless this is directly stated to the reader by a third party. The very signature where the & was placed is a bold move in that it comes very close to do this, since signing something is the same as telling directly that it comes from you, but by changing the 3 letters to & it makes the identity of its owner into a conclusion, and the reader was rendered unable to reach the conclusion that the letter came from Anderson.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After multiple reads, I'm still confused as to what this skip does or even is if someone can help me wrap my head around this it'll be greatly appreciated

item.title: Re: +51
item.content:encoded: Can't wait! :)
Wow, when did this hit 50? Thanks all. I've been rewriting this skip for about three years, give or take a half-month or two. One day, I'll post it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: All but the last are fine. I think the last should stay a bonus; we sort of agreed on that before during writing it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Authors may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so this article makes a lot of direct references to things with pages on the site including: MTF Gamma-13, Anderson Robotics, The UIU, and SCP-1781. Honestly, its a simple matter of just making these into inline links, and bam, you have yourself a wikiwalk. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

The identity of SCP-2906-1, or its creator, are to be identified as soon as possible and monitored for future anomalous activity. After the events of Incident PoI-1115-C, CTF Psi-12 is to work in conjunction with Mobile Task Force Gamma-13 ("Asimov's Lawbringers") to contain SCP-2906 and coordinate efforts in apprehending PoI-1115 ("Anderson").

To this:

The identity of SCP-2906-1, or its creator, are to be identified as soon as possible and monitored for future anomalous activity. After the events of Incident PoI-1115-C, CTF Psi-12 is to work in conjunction with Mobile Task Force Gamma-13 ("Asimov's Lawbringers") to contain SCP-2906 and coordinate efforts in apprehending PoI-1115 ("Anderson").

Changing this:

Interviewed: Agents Lily Rondeau (Foundation) and Bernard Zell (Unusual Incidents Unit) of CTF Psi-12, Agent Sasha Merlo of MTF Gamma-13

To this:

Interviewed: Agents Lily Rondeau (Foundation) and Bernard Zell (Unusual Incidents Unit) of CTF Psi-12, Agent Sasha Merlo of MTF Gamma-13

Changing this:

P.S.: Let me know if my new friends managed to get back to that drive-in theater in Indiana before you!

To this:

P.S.: Let me know if my new friends managed to get back to that drive-in theater in Indiana before you!

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really do love that letter at the end. It's so quintessentially Anderson: affable, chipper, and deeply, deeply sinister.

item.title: Translations
item.content:encoded:
To promote translations of my work and thank all the authors for their hard work, I am linking to them here in addition to my author page.

  • Chinese by qjfoidnh
  • Japanese by C-Dives
  • Portuguese by Silvacrest
  • Russian by MobButcher
  • Spanish by saugonzo

Thank you all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) The Foundation's version of the infohazard is easy enough to modify that a person/group with a focus on robotics was able to manage it - which means that every GoI could do the same, and this one is dangerous.

You're right in that the Foundation's original use of the infohazard wasn't well-secured enough for other groups to modify it once they knew what to look for, which is the important thing. It's not that the Foundation didn't try to make it secure, but they didn't anticipate how it'd get broken.

2) Anderson is highly skilled at creating infohazards, which is well outside their established market.

Well, Anderson didn't actually make it; he got by from a little help with his friends (J&F specifically). A story in which it would be detailed how Jakob and Finn came into contact with Anderson in the first place sort of fell by the wayside once TwistedGears stopped using the site, but we have our ideas.

The rewrite will have an MC&D GoI Format which details how MC&D and Anderson came into contact and allowed MC&D to use it. I will also say; Anderson doesn't intend to use this infohazard for the long-run. It's just to give his company enough of a headstart over the Foundation to continue doing what they do (and it kinda works; see Conwell's tale series).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with Petrograd on this one - the Foundation uses anomalous items when required, but this seems like a pointless use, and implies one of two things:

1) The Foundation's version of the infohazard is easy enough to modify that a person/group with a focus on robotics was able to manage it - which means that every GoI could do the same, and this one is dangerous.

2) Anderson is highly skilled at creating infohazards, which is well outside their established market.

I could buy a group like AWCY? doing this purely for the art, I could see the Serpent's Hand or Chaos Insurgency doing it as a weapon, and I could see MC&D doing it (while immunizing their clients) to maintain privacy. It really doesn't work for me with Anderson, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad to hear that. While it is brief, your portrayal of Merlo is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, thanks! That last bit was intentional.

Although, this SCP is currently undergoing a rewrite by TwistedGears and I, which may not have an interview anymore, but I'm not gonna retire these characters, that's for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea that the SCP acronym in Foundation front companies is significant, I'm going to have to look up Learning the Alphabet if that's where that came from.

Additionally, I like how Bernard Zell says "skip". He's spent enough time with the Foundation that he's picking up their slang :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love cognitohazards and I love this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it's just that you're sleep-deprived, unfortunately. I thought that was the case with the clarity for me as well when I read it last night, so I put it aside to read again today and found that I did still have to go over parts a couple times to really make sense of the whole thing.

I have to agree with your previous comment, as well as with Doctor Cimmerian when he says, "There's a lot here to describe a very simple idea, and it doesn't feel like it pays off the effort to read it." However, if the authors are up for the challenge, I do think there is a lot of promise in a slight reconceptualizing of this, more than enough to keep me from voting just yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I tend to go with the "Stand on it's own" principle when reviewing, but I'm not opposed to linking and usually I wouldn't call it out. In this case it was just frustrating, because I really liked the note (it's pretty awesome!), and was trying to draw a connection with the article, but (I'm guessing) without reading the tale I just couldn't quite find the strings. I'll probably click the link and read it, I just didn't while reviewing.

That's an issue for me (similar with the clarity, it's possible I'm just sleep-deprived), but I'm voting mostly on the central anomaly and its origins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While you make a lot of valid points that I can at least understand (and TG would be better able to answer RE: Foundation using their acronym as a cognitohazard, given he wrote that in a tale prior), there is one I can address:

and sections that rely on external content to their detriment

If you mean anything to do with the linking, then they're meant to be more universe-building and connecting than "you must read this in order to understand this". I could blackbox the SCP and remove the link to the tale, but I wanted to build on top of what Conwell has provided and give his stuff attention. Though, in a way, if it still doesn't work even if I removed those references, then it doesn't work as a stand-alone and there's a problem there.

Thank you for taking the time to read and comment. c:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm… not really on board with this one, for a variety of reasons. Minor disclaimer, I've read some but not all of the Anderson tales/SCPs, and I didn't/don't intend to click any of the links in this article.

Ok, here we go.

+ show block
– hide block

On the first couple readthroughs, I had trouble picturing the concept, particularly what it would look like to an affected person. Even after groking that, I'm still not clear on the transmission characteristics. The interview log was an interesting exploration of the consequences in a specific scenario, but it didn't really help me with either of these issues. The toy-box analogy, for example, didn't really clarify it at all.

The Foundation-originating cognitohazard… Well, it does makes sense. We definitely see the Foundation using memetic-kill images left and right, and we know these obvious Foundation fronts can operate openly, so why not have them use cognitihazards? Plus, the way this one is set up sounds great… until you think about it.

The problem is, the way this is implemented is incredibly stupid (in-universe) for several reasons:
1) You're deploying something that is inherently-amnestic resistant and incredibly easily spread on people who are… who? Members of the public asking the wrong questions? Deliberate infiltrators who are aware of the Foundation, and thus most likely part of a GoI? I just don't ever see a use case where releasing a ravenous infohazard erasing both connections with and the idea of the Foundation is a good idea. Unless you think you can give it to the West Coast Fifthists every month and hope it doesn't cross-contaminate the GOC, UIU, or U.S. government.
I mean, it prefect for Anderson, but who on earth were the Foundation going to use this on?
2) It's simple enough to modify that (assuming you can perceive it or copy the text) you can smudge out SCP and write anything. I mean, Jesus Christ people.
3) It's perfectly understood (they wrote it), but anomalous enough that when it's not being used by the Foundation, it's considered an SCP. This falls somewhere outside of the categories of demi-anomalous tech (e.g., amnestics, GOC Orange Suits) and secret anomaly use (Thaumiels) into a territory of double-standard I'm not comfortable with. If it was clear that Anderson had modified its effects in any way, maybe. But it's not.
4) How are the Foundation members immune to their version? What if they want to recruit someone already infected?

Ultimately, I could maybe see this deployed as a bureaucratic and all-around terrible idea and later revoked, and then ripped off by Anderson. Maybe. I think it's interesting enough that it wouldn't just be wacky incompetent Foundation hijinks. Unfortunately, that's not explored. We're just supposed to take it at face value that the secretary at Smith & Pool Consultants has 40 of these in their desk.

Also, the final letter. On the upside, it's one of my favorite Foundation-kiss-off notes ever, which is impressive because I usually detest them. On the downside, in the context of the article, I have absolutely no idea what any of it means. As I said, for this article, I'm not clicking links or reading tales, so the note for me has no meaning whatsoever. I'm not talking positive things like an air of mystery or an enigmatic character showing off. It's like a postcard from someone I've never met happened to fall on my SCP dossier.

Honestly, I think I'm gonna downvote. It relies too much on an incredibly flawed central premise (a Foundation device useless to the Foundation but prefect for Anderson), some clarity issues, and sections that rely on external content to their detriment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad someone liked the MTF name :3 References like that are what make writing fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All due respect to Doctor Cimmerian, but I think how this was handled- a hat-tip kind of reference to a cognitohazard used by the Foundation- was perfect. The article is about an exterior threat: the focus shouldn't be on the Foundation. Though it would be awesome to see the idea fleshed out more fully in a tale. Also, upvoted at least partially for 'MTF PSI-12 ("Madvillainy").'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda felt like the interview is just reiterating the description. I mean, it's pretty clear what's going on, reading four people discussing it at length didn't add anything. No-vote for now, though I otherwise like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huzzah! You finally did the thing!

I'm not going to pretend like I don't have a bias in upvoting this article. It takes the idea of the Anderson PoI and moves it in a neat direction that I never considered, and also ties in Gamma-13 and the UIU, which is one of my favorite GoIs on the site.

+1 guys. Glad to finally see this on the site.

Also, icwatudidthar with the number choice. Very clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How do you mean "related to"? If you mean if this was inspired by it, some inspiration for the idea certainly came from that, yeah.

EDIT: TG ninja yeaaaaaaaaaaaaa

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Among a variety of other things, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this at all related to the thing in TG's Learning the Alphabet series where people (specifically Aldon) are unable to figure out Spicy Crust Pizza is tied to the Foundation until it's spelled out for them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the concept here is pretty simple really. Though it's very hard to describe. That means that if you're gonna execute it well, you need to make double damn sure that the reader understands what it is very quickly so you can dive into the story you want to tell. I went far too long into reading this without understanding it. Which normally is fine if the concept is something really really interesting and gives me an "aha" moment. But this doesn't do that.

The idea is something that we've seen on the site in multiple skips. There's a bit of a twist on the concept, but nothing outstanding. There's a lot here to describe a very simple idea, and it doesn't feel like it pays off the effort to read it.

The first two sentences of the description, as an example, reference the SCP Foundation using a version of this thing. That's a huge deal. But you don't expand on that. You just say it, and then the story moves to focus on Anderson. The last thing you want to do with a concept this hard to describe is introduce red herrings. Because now you got my mind focused on "Wait, did that just say the Foundation is using this sort of thing to preserve secrecy? How does that work?".

I think, to be frank and honest here, that your best story thread is the Foundation, not Anderson. Of course an GOI or POI is using it this way. There's nothing surprising about that. But the Foundation creating/using something like this that then gets used by others and then gets out of control? That could hook my interest.

As it stands, this is bland and it meanders. The concept doesn't carry the piece. The Anderson tie-ins don't add to the skip and they don't feel like they add to Anderson. They're just there.

The dialogue works for me. It felt natural, actually maybe a little too casual but that's a personal preference. I don't like strikethroughs but we talked about that in chat. I am eager to see this improved upon, because it has grand potential. Good luck with the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That interview log is my favorite part in this article. Anyway, seeing the Foundation working with third parties does seem weird. But it was executed perfectly in my opinion.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
A collaboration between TwistedGears and I! We both got stuck with getting feedback from others so after a certain point of time, we just decided to take what we had and put it up on the site. We hope you enjoy it, and ask for any feedback you give on this!

Thanks to Conwell, Crs_to_Baal, and thedeadlymoose and others (who's names I've forgotten) for feedback on this.
